title: Building Logex: When Every Session Deserves to Become a Blog Post
summary: Memex and session-recap were humming along nicely when I suddenly realized each session was worth preserving—not as memo cards, but as proper blog posts. So I ported the bidirectional linking logic over, named it Engram (then changed it to Logex because it sounded better), packaged it into a GitHub repo for storage, and ended up giving myself a brutal UI critique.
body:
## The Spark: A "Sudden Realization"

That evening I already had Memex and session-recap running. Memex was storing my daily insights as Zettelkasten notes—atomic, with `[[links]]`, English kebab-case slugs; session-recap was summarizing each Claude Code session into cards. Both worked great.

Then I was staring at that pile of `*.jsonl` files under `~/.claude/projects/`, and it hit me:

> Every session deserves to have its story properly preserved, becoming part of my future memory, or brain.

Memex cards were too short—insights, not process. Session-recap summaries were too short—indexes, not stories. The real value lived in that middle layer: what a session accomplished, what pitfalls it hit, what turns it took, why we decided what we decided.

This wasn't a memo. This was a **blog**.

## First Decision: One-to-One or Many-to-Many

Initially I thought `session → article` one-to-one. Then I killed that idea after two seconds:

> Each session doesn't necessarily map to one article—could be multiple. Because in one session we might tackle several things. Ideally when I detect topic switches, it auto-generates separate pieces.

So the first design decision locked in: **LLM-driven segmentation**. Not cutting by time, not by tokens, not by tool-use. Feed the entire session to the LLM and let it recognize "what's this section about" and "did we just change topics here." One session could yield 0 articles, 1 article, or 5 articles.

This decision proved right later. My day-spanning sessions often went: morning auth bug debugging, lunch frontend redesign, afternoon UX review with PM+designer, evening back to backend fixes. Hard cuts would shred complete debugging stories into fragments.

## Pipeline Shape

After several iterations, the pipeline looks like this:

```
JSONL → parse.ts → chunk.ts (score) → prepare.ts
         ↓
     chunkSummaries[] + segmentationPrompt
         ↓
     LLM (Claude in session) → groups[]
         ↓
     article writing (LLM 自己写)
         ↓
     publish.ts (idempotent upsert)
         ↓
     logex-data repo → Vercel → logex.vercel.app
```

Two key points:

1. **Claude writes articles within the session itself**—no API calls, no API key costs, just using the current Claude's own context and token budget. This aligns with Memex's design philosophy: you're already an LLM, don't spawn another LLM to boss yourself around.
2. **prepare.ts only does pure computation**—parse, chunk, score, roughly 2 seconds. All judgment work (is this a topic? worth writing about?) gets delegated to the LLM.

## Bidirectional Links: Reusing Memex Logic

User quote: "Fundamentally it still needs memex's essence—bidirectional links."

So Logex articles naturally carry `[[slug]]` syntax, and the frontend renders them by scanning and connecting as it goes. Two articles mentioning the same concept automatically backlink each other. I didn't invent this—Obsidian, Logseq, Andy Matuschak's notes all work this way. But being able to port it directly from Memex saved me two days.

Good enough. No need for graph DB, no need for embedding search. I'll add those when reading ergonomics demands it.

## Storage: GitHub Repo or Database?

This was the longest decision struggle. Two options:

- **GitHub repo** (flat files + git): free version history, no hosting costs, same logic as Memex, users can fork
- **Database** (Supabase / Postgres): fast queries, strong search, auth and multi-user support

I asked myself: who are logex's users?

**Right now there's one user—me.** Future might open source it for others. All the database advantages—auth, multi-user, concurrent writes—are useless now. All the GitHub repo disadvantages—slow queries, weak search—don't matter under 100 articles.

So I decided: **GitHub repo as primary, but leave DB adapter for the future.** Runtime reads `VITE_STORAGE` env to choose adapter. Production uses `VITE_STORAGE=github`, pointing to `iamtouchskyer/logex-data`. That repo is the data layer, app is the view layer, clean separation.

## Naming: Engram → Logex

For a while I called it **Engram** (neuroscience term for "memory trace"). Pretty pretentious. User feedback "doesn't sound good, can you find something like memex, memra" shot it down immediately.

`mem-ex` = memory + index. `log-ex` = log + index. Parallel structure, flows well, forms a family with Memex. Done.

This naming decision is actually typical: **don't fall in love with your first idea.** Engram was meaningful but hard to pronounce; Logex wasn't as "deep" but memorable. Memorable > profound—especially when you'll be typing it 100 times in terminal.

## Publish Gotcha: Where Should Idempotency Live in the Pipeline

First version publish was just `append to index.json`. Result: rerunning `/logex` double-inserted—same session's same article got written twice.

Three fix approaches:

1. **Articles carry sessionId + chunkIndices** → `publish.ts` reads existing index, recognizes same article by (sessionId, chunkIndices), updates instead of appending
2. **Let LLM decide update vs insert**—but LLM decisions drift
3. **Unique slug = hash(sessionId + chunkIndices)**—mechanical dedup

Ended up combining 1 + 2: `publish.ts prepare-match` first uses code to fast-path sessionId matching, direct insert if unambiguous; only throws to LLM for ambiguous cases (same session rerun with different chunk grouping). Two-layer idempotency makes `/logex` reruns safe.

## UI: Getting Roasted by Myself

MVP launch day I opened it myself: three-column sidebar, white background, an article list, done. Functionality was correct, looked like a 2005 wiki.

Then I went to my OPC (One-Person Company) system and said:

> /opc please spin up PM and designer to properly design this thing

PM agent wrote me a PRD: homepage needs timeline, tag cloud, search, dark mode, reading progress. Designer agent drew layouts. I spent one evening implementing, homepage became:

- Left sidebar: tag cloud + project grouping
- Center: article timeline (grouped by date)
- Right: current article reader + backlinks panel

Same information density, completely different visual "breathing room."

**Takeaway**: finishing E2E isn't the end. Open it, use it for 5 minutes, roast it, then fix it. Frontend needs this step more than backend, because UI "correctness" isn't something tests can catch.

## Three Decisions That Were Right in Hindsight

1. **LLM writes articles itself, no API calls**—cheaper, faster, complete context
2. **GitHub repo for storage, but keep DB adapter**—lightweight now, no refactoring later
3. **Port bidirectional links from Memex**—don't invent, reuse what already works

An idea went from "sudden realization" to running in about two days. The longest stretch wasn't coding—it was naming. Good naming deserves that time investment.

Now every session ends with `/logex`, and `logex.vercel.app` gets a few more articles, automatically becoming part of my own brain. Just like this one.
